definition: the process of formally receiving records into an archive and establishing initial physical and intellectual control over them.
the process of evaluating records to determine their long-term value and whether they should be preserved or disposed of.
the process of creating structured representations of archival materials, including cataloguing and the production of finding aids that explain context, scope, and access conditions.
an aggregation of archival materials assembled by provenance or subject, often forming the highest level of description in archival hierarchies.
information describing the origins, creators, and circumstances of records, forming a core component of archival description standards such as ISAD(G).
a rules-based standard for describing archives and manuscripts, representing the US implementation of international standards such as ISAD(G) and ISAAR(CPF).

term: EAC-CPF (Encoded Archival Context – Corporate Bodies, Persons and Families)
definition: an XML schema used to encode contextual information about the creators of archival materials, including organisations, individuals, and families.
an XML-based standard for encoding archival finding aids, enabling structured, machine-readable descriptions of archival collections.
a structured descriptive document (e.g. inventory or guide) providing information on the scope, content, and context of archival materials to support discovery and access.
the whole of the records created and accumulated by a particular organisation, family, or individual, forming the highest level of archival description.
the General International Standard Archival Description, providing a framework of 26 elements across seven areas to enable consistent, multi-level description of archival materials.
an international standard for describing archival authority records relating to corporate bodies, persons, and families associated with archival materials.
a hierarchical method of description moving from general (fonds) to specific (item), ensuring relationships between levels are maintained without duplication.
the practice of preserving records in the arrangement established by their creator, maintaining evidential context and relationships.
the ongoing care and maintenance of archival materials through appropriate storage, handling, and environmental controls to ensure long-term accessibility.
the origin or source of records, including the creator and custodial history, forming the basis for archival arrangement and description.
recorded information created or received in the course of activity, possessing content, context, and structure sufficient to serve as evidence.
a body of records created by a specific organisation or administrative unit, often used in governmental archival systems.
the rule that records from different creators must not be intermingled, maintaining the integrity of their provenance.
a summary describing the subject matter, form, and arrangement of archival materials at a given level of description.
a group of similar records within a fonds, typically organised by function, subject, or activity, forming a level within the archival hierarchy.
the process by which records are moved from their creator to an archival repository, often as part of accessioning and custody management.
any level within an archival hierarchy (fonds, series, file, item) that is described as part of a structured archival system.
